title: Type 3 Control Channel
established: Separating control from data — evolved into unified message protocol

title: Push-Phase Memoization
established: RESOLVED signal for transitive skip — carried forward

title: Explicit Deps
No-Default-Dedup
established: Operators transparent by default — carried forward

title: Output Slot
Lazy Tier 2
established: get() doesn't guarantee freshness — evolved into status-based trust

title: Promise Elimination
established: Internal APIs return sources not Promises — carried forward

title: Vision: LLM Actuator
established: Three-layer vision, Graph as universal output — directly triggered GraphReFly

title: Unified Spec: Protocol, Single Primitive, Graph Container
topic: Designing the GraphReFly unified cross-repo spec through a 7-step process. Radical simplification from callbag-recharge's 6 primitives + 4 callbag types to 1 primitive (node) + unified message format.
decisions: One primitive: node(deps?, fn?, opts?) — behavior from configuration, sugar constructors for readability
Unified message format: always [[Type, Data?], ...], 9 types, no channel separation
Unified node interface: .get() (cached, never errors), .status, .down(), .up(), .unsubscribe(), .meta
Meta as companion stores — each key subscribable, replaces all with*() wrappers
No separate Knob/Gauge/Inspector — describe() + observe() on Graph
Pure wire edges — no transforms, everything is a node
Colon namespace — "system:payment:validate"

title: Web3 Integration Sketch: Security, OMS, Agent Commerce
topic: Research into how GraphReFly maps onto Web3 security monitoring ($3.4B lost in 2025), AI agent commerce (x402/ERC-8004/ERC-8183), and the missing reactive order management layer for decentralized systems.
decisions: GraphReFly needs no new primitives for Web3 — just adapters (fromChainEvents, fromX402, toTransaction) and domain graph factories
GraphReFly runs off-chain as the coordination brain; contracts stay on-chain
Three detailed graph topology sketches: security monitor, cross-chain order lifecycle, multi-agent job workflow
Custom message types leverage the open message type set (spec §1.2)

title: Message Type Extensibility Analysis
topic: Evaluating whether TS and Python type definitions allow users to define and emit custom message types, as required by the spec's open message type set.
decisions: TypeScript: open — Message = readonly [symbol, unknown?] accepts any symbol
Python: closed — Message = tuple[MessageType, Any] restricts to StrEnum. Needs widening to MessageType | str
Smart contract language comparison (Solidity, Rust, Move, Vyper, Cairo) and why GraphReFly is off-chain

title: Built-in ABAC: Actor, Guard, Policy Builder
topic: Designing built-in access control for GraphReFly that replaces external authz libraries (e.g. CASL). The graph is the single enforcement point.
decisions: Three primitives: Actor context (who), capability guard (may they), scoped introspection (what can they see)
policy() declarative builder — CASL-style allow()/deny() DX, zero dependencies, ~50 LOC
Attribution pulled to Phase 1.5 — node.lastMutation records { actor, timestamp }
CASL rejected as dependency — its subject model, sift.js, pack/unpack unnecessary when graph is only enforcement point
Web3 identity maps cleanly — wallet signatures, x402 proofs, ERC-8004 agent IDs all produce actors
roadmap_impact: New Phase 1.5 (Actor & Guard), expanded Phase 1.6 (tests), Phase 5.4 accepts actor?, Phase 6 simplified

title: Spec Compliance, Patterns, callbag-recharge Parity, Tooling Gaps
topic: Structured 16-batch audit of graphrefly-ts and graphrefly-py against GRAPHREFLY-SPEC.md, internal API consistency, callbag-recharge lessons, operator/data-structure edge cases, and AI-facing introspection.
decisions: Phase A (batches 1-3): strong PASS on core protocol, node, and Graph; open TS vs Py batch-drain deferral inconsistency
Phases B-C (4-8): pattern and pitfall audits; documented fixes
Batch 13: superset-deps pattern correct but may recompute on irrelevant dep changes
Batch 14: RxJS alignment mostly good; naming divergences for LLM ergonomics
Batch 15: describe()/observe() insufficient for causal debugging; proposes structured inspect

title: Reactive Issue Tracker: Dogfooding GraphReFly for Project Management
topic: Designing a reactive issue tracker / agentic knowledge graph that uses graphrefly-ts to solve its own project management pain.
decisions: Issues are live assertions, not status cards — every issue carries a verify() function
Fixed ≠ verified — the graph re-runs verifiers and only transitions to verified on evidence
Regression detection via reactive propagation — file/git changes trigger re-verification
Typed relationships (affects, blockedBy, relatedTo) enable graph queries
AI-native via observe() + annotate() — agents subscribe, get pushed updates

title: Tier 2 Operator Parity: forwardInner, nonlocal, sample Architecture
topic: Cross-repo parity analysis and fixes for three divergences in Python's Tier 2 operators.
decisions: _forward_inner emitted flag — prevents double-DATA when inner emits during subscribe
nonlocal replaces [value] list-boxing — idiomatic Python 3 across all 18 operators
sample rewritten to dep+onMessage — eliminates mirror node
Tier 2 regression testing is the top testing priority

title: Seamless Snapshot/Hydration: Auto-Checkpoint & Node Factory Registry
topic: Designing zero-friction resume for dynamic graphs — auto-checkpoint and node factory registry for fromSnapshot reconstruction.
decisions: Auto-checkpoint fires after settlement — filter to DATA/RESOLVED (phase-2 messages), debounce ~500ms
Incremental snapshots via Graph.diff() — reduces I/O from O(graph_size) to O(changed_nodes)
Factory registry by name glob pattern — not by node type or custom meta field
Guards reconstruct from data — policyFromRules(snap.value.rules) rebuilds guard fns
Topological reconstruction order — mounts → state/producer → derived/operator/effect → edges → restore values

title: Demo & Test Strategy: 4 Demos, GraphReFly-Powered Shell, Reactive Layout, Inspection-as-Test-Harness
topic: Designing the demo and test strategy for Phase 4 domain layers. Four demos each exercising 3+ domain layers with 10-12 numbered acceptance criteria.
decisions: Three-pane shell IS a GraphReFly graph — shell bugs are library bugs
Reactive layout engine (Pretext-on-GraphReFly) — DOM-free text measurement as state → derived pipeline
Acceptance criteria prevent descoping — 10-12 specific, testable ACs per demo
Inspection layer IS the test harness — observe(), describe(), toMermaid() simultaneously showcases, stress-tests, validates
Four-layer test strategy — unit, scenario, inspection stress, adversarial
Eight foreseen building blocks
Non-LLM demos first — Demo 1 and 3 ship before Demo 2 and 4
WebLLM for browser demos — Qwen3 via WebGPU, zero API keys
roadmap_impact: New Phase 7.1-7.6 in TS repo, 7.1-7.4 in PY repo

title: Agentic Memory SOTA Synthesis + Default agentMemory() Strategy
topic: Synthesizing SOTA agentic memory research into a concrete default strategy for agentMemory() (Phase 4.4).
decisions: Four strategies map to existing primitives — 3D filtering funnel, GraphRAG, dynamic reflection, hot/cold tiers
agentMemory() is composition, not a new primitive — wires distill() + knowledgeGraph() + vectorIndex() + collection() + decay() + autoCheckpoint()
Default strategy is opinionated but overridable — OpenViking decay formula
LLM extraction/consolidation stays in userland adapters, never inside core
Five unique advantages over all existing systems

title: Universal Reduction Layer: Massive Info → Actionable Items via LLM-Composable Reactive Graphs
topic: Generalizing GraphReFly from domain-specific tools to a universal reactive reduction layer for any massive info → actionable items pattern.
decisions: GraphReFly is not an observability tool or an issue tracker — it's the reactive computation layer
Three things LLMs unlock: composing the graph, operating inside nodes, auditing decisions
Stratified reduction is the killer differentiator
CICD moment for information processing
10 concrete advantages documented

title: Adoption Blockers: Memory Footprint, DAG-CBOR Codec, Tiered Representation, NodeV0 Promotion
topic: Investigating practical blockers for adoption at scale: runtime memory, serialization overhead, hydration latency.
decisions: DAG-CBOR as default codec — replaces JSON for wire/checkpoint, ~40-50% smaller
Tiered representation — hot (JS objects), warm (DAG-CBOR + lazy hydration), cold (Arrow/Parquet), peek (FlatBuffers)
Five memory tuning strategies — lazy meta, structural sharing, bounded history, struct-of-arrays, dormant eviction
Delta checkpoints more impactful than any format choice — ~400x smaller than full snapshots at steady state
NodeV0 should move from Phase 6 to Phase 3.x — minimum enabler for delta checkpoints, wire-efficient sync

title: Marketing, Promotion & Domain Strategy
topic: Comprehensive marketing strategy for GraphReFly — package descriptions, domain setup, Cloudflare, site architecture, AI discoverability, callbag-recharge deprecation, and phased promotion roadmap.
decisions: Single Starlight site per language — graphrefly.dev (TS) and py.graphrefly.dev (PY)
Three positioning pillars — The Missing Middle, Durable by Default, Inspectable AI Orchestration
AI-permissive crawling — robots.txt allowing GPTBot/ClaudeBot/PerplexityBot + llms.txt
Phased promotion — Foundation → Soft Launch → Growth → Sustained
callbag-recharge deprecation — npm deprecate, README banner, blog migration
Public asset pipeline — robots.txt and llms.txt at repo root, synced by sync-docs.mjs

title: First-Principles Audit: Is GraphReFly Necessary?
topic: Deep self-examination of whether GraphReFly is over-engineered, starting from first principles.
decisions: GraphReFly is not over-engineered — optimized for human+LLM reactive co-operation bet
Irreducible core is five things: node, edge, propagation, describe/snapshot, GraphSpec
Graph > functions for LLM composition — constrains error space like SQL vs hand-written DB ops
Causal chain persistence is the killer argument — not reactivity, not performance
No LoRA/SFT needed if GraphSpec stays simple enough for zero-shot
Demo 0 (The Existential Demo) designed: NL → GraphSpec → simplified flow → run → persist → causal explain
Three-layer DX/UX strategy: LLM-DX, Dev-DX, End-user UX — Layer 3 is the real moat
Reposition: lead with user pain points, not architecture
research_findings: Callbag (2018) had near-identical thesis, died from zero ecosystem
Industry converged on Signals for sync, Observables for async
Virtual threads killed server-side reactive in Java; coordination-protocol positioning remains viable
HN feedback: sounds good but don't understand the solution — confirms architecture-first messaging fails

title: Competitive Analysis: DeerFlow 2.0 & Deep Agents vs GraphReFly
topic: Research-backed comparison of DeerFlow 2.0 (ByteDance, 32k stars) and LangChain Deep Agents (19.3k stars) against GraphReFly.
decisions: DeerFlow's 1.0→2.0 evolution trajectory points at GraphReFly — steps 4-7 already provided
DeerFlow is a harness, GraphReFly is a protocol — different abstraction layers, composable
File system as state is 2026 consensus but has fundamental limits
Tool-as-Agent vs Topology-as-Program is the core philosophical divide
Deep Agents validates the pattern without depth
Complementary is market entry tactic, not architectural truth
Ecosystem infiltration via 3 entry points: MCP Server, workspace bridge, LangGraph adapter

title: Harness Engineering Gap Analysis, Three-Wave Announcement Plan, Eval System Design, Ecosystem Infiltration
topic: Research-backed gap analysis between harness engineering (2026's defining trend) and GraphReFly's existing architecture.
decisions: Anti-stealth: moat is category ownership, not code secrecy. Window April-June 2026
Three announcement waves: Wave 1 (eval), Wave 2 (harness layer + MCP + scorecard), Wave 3 (Demo 0 + Show HN)
Two-tier eval: portable/manual + automated/API. LLMProvider interface for model switching
MCP Server as primary infiltration vector — one npm package reaches all MCP clients
explainPath is THE differentiator — causal walkback, P0 priority
Reposition from reactive graph engine to reactive harness layer for agent workflows
roadmap_impact: New §9.1-9.7 in TS roadmap, §9.0/9.2/9.2b in PY roadmap, §15 in marketing strategy

title: Reactive Collaboration Harness: Static-Topology Loop for Human+LLM Co-operation
topic: Designing a 7-stage static-topology loop (intake → triage → queue → gate → execute → verify → reflect) for human+LLM co-operation.
decisions: Static topology + flowing data — graph models the workflow (pipes), not work items (water)
gate.modify() IS structured human judgment — ported from callbag-recharge with Array.map-style signature
Cursor reading as dimensionality reduction — converts exponential graph branching into linear sequence
promptNode factory — universal LLM transform: any prompt + deps → processing node
Strategy model (intervention effectiveness) — derived node tracking rootCause×intervention → successRate
distill() kept for memory, not replaced by promptNode
Existing infrastructure covers ~70% — new work: gate port (~200 LOC), promptNode (~100 LOC), valve rename, wiring (~430 LOC)
Boolean gate → valve rename — frees gate for the real human-approval primitive
Build as §9.0 before 9.1b — dogfood the loop while running the 4-treatment eval experiment

title: Inspection Tool Consolidation: 14+ Tools → 9, TS + PY Parity
topic: Reducing inspection tool surface from 14+ exports to 9 non-overlapping tools across TS and PY. Triggered by debugging PY gate.modify() test exposing tool sprawl and invariant violations.
decisions: spy() merges into observe(format=) — spy is observe+logger, no independent logic
annotate()+traceLog() merge into trace() — one method, write+read overloads
4 RxJS bridge functions consolidate to single toObservable(source, opts?)
describeNode/metaSnapshot unexported — internal plumbing for describe()
Polling _wait_for replaced by filter|take(1) reactive composition — no new primitive needed
harnessTrace() added — wires observe() to all 7 harness stages for pipeline visibility
Graph.diff() ported to PY — snapshot comparison
Runner __repr__ added — pending task counter for diagnostic dumps
Pre-1.0: breaking changes, no aliases or legacy shims
invariants_enforced: §5.9 No imperative triggers — harnessTrace surfaces reactive flow gaps
§5.10 No raw Promises — Runner repr shows missing runner
§5.12 Dev-friendly — 9 tools vs 14+ reduces cognitive load
pending: PY gate.modify() test needs re-validation with new inspection tools

title: Mid-Level Harness Blocks: graphLens, resilientPipeline, guardedExecution, persistentState
topic: Designing composed building blocks between raw primitives and harnessLoop() — the missing mid-level layer for power users building custom harness variants.
decisions: Three-layer API stack: primitives → mid-level blocks → harnessLoop()
graphLens() — observability as reactive nodes (topology, health, flow, why), never natural language
resilientPipeline() — encodes correct nesting order (rateLimiter→breaker→retry→timeout→fallback)
guardedExecution() — ABAC + policy + budgetGate as composable safety wrapper
persistentState() — autoCheckpoint + snapshot + restore + incremental diff bundled
Four blocks not eight — 8 requirements cluster naturally, some already standalone (gate, agentMemory)
Library never generates natural language — structured data only, LLMs/UIs render
roadmap_impact: New §9.0b in TS roadmap, between §9.0 (collaboration loop) and Wave 1 (eval story)

title: Inspection Harness Revalidation: Subscription Ordering, Fire-and-Forget Semantics, Test Pattern Corrections
topic: Post-consolidation revalidation using new inspection tools. Fixed subscription-ordering bugs in test replacements, established correct patterns for fire-and-forget semantics (wire before emit, .get() for cached state).
decisions: first_where uses direct subscriber not operator chain — filter→take→firstValueFrom fails with synchronous sources
_wait_for polling replaced by three patterns: .get() for cached, subscribe-before-emit for future, threading.Event for async status
TraceEvent structured output with progressive detail levels (summary/standard/full), default summary
COMPOSITION-GUIDE §2 mandatory for all patterns/ work — updated dev-dispatch/qa/parity skills
Category A (move protocol ops to extra/) and Category B (.set() sugar) added as roadmap follow-ups

title: Protocol Semantics Revalidation: Push/Pull at Connection, Fire-and-Forget Foundations, Operator Chain Composition
topic: Deep examination of GraphReFly connection-time semantics. Verified state is inert at connection (no push, no pull), derived pushes DIRTY+DATA on first activation only, no pull mechanism exists. Fixed 12 test failures from incorrect first_where usage. Documented operator chain composition pitfall.
decisions: state is inert at connection — no push, no pull, cached value only via .get()
derived pushes DIRTY+DATA on first subscriber only — side effect of activation, not replay
No pull-at-connection exists — up() is never called during subscribe
filter→take→first_value_from fails because synchronous data flows through chain during wiring before terminal subscriber attaches
Mock adapters make pipeline synchronous — use .get() after publish, not reactive waits
open_questions: Should state push its value on subscribe?
Should COMPOSITION-GUIDE document operator chain composition pitfall?
Is derived 1st-vs-2nd subscriber asymmetry intentional?
Should protocol have a PULL/REQUEST message type?

title: Connection-Time Diamond, Subscribe-Time Push, SENTINEL Gating — Phase 5 LLM Validation Fallout
topic: Phase 5 LLM composition validation exposed spec-impl gaps in the push model. Fixed connection-time diamond resolution (spec §2.7) via structural invariant in _onDepSettled. Fixed derived/producer double-delivery on first subscribe (subscribe-time push with cachedBefore snapshot). Added SENTINEL indicator to describe(). Composition guide §9 (diamonds + two-phase) and §10 (SENTINEL vs null-guard cascade) added. Session context degraded mid-work — several wrong claims were made and reverted, and 4-5 tests in the new semantic-audit file enshrine incorrect behavior that must be fixed in a fresh session.
decisions: Q1 (user decided YES, not yet implemented): _onDepSettled must defer fn until ALL deps have produced a real value at least once — per composition guide §1 — SENTINEL deps gate the initial run
Q2 (user confirmed): multiple DIRTY per propagation wave is spec-allowed — current per-dep wasDirty check is correct
Q3 (pending): reconnect re-run semantics — RxJS shareReplay({refCount:true}) alignment proposed (clear _lastDepValues on disconnect, keep _cached)
get() never triggers computation (spec §2.2 line 206) — a reverted fix violated this
Connection-time diamond fix uses structural invariant (_upstreamUnsubs.length < _deps.length) — no flags
Subscribe-time push skip uses cachedBefore snapshot — derived/producer no longer double-deliver first value
Producer tests previously amended to [42,42] in f34d71e restored to single delivery
open_questions: Q1 impl: add _depEverSettledMask OR boolean _depsAllInitialized OR internal _cached check
Q3 decision: match RxJS shareReplay({refCount:true}) re-run semantics?
DynamicNodeImpl lazy-dep composition — how should dynamicNode handle reading from lazy derived (get() cannot auto-activate per spec)
DynamicNodeImpl vs NodeImpl code duplication — should DynamicNodeImpl extend NodeImpl?
Effect reconnect — different from derived due to cleanup lifecycle?
must_fix_in_fresh_session: src/__tests__/core/semantic-audit.test.ts:276,296 — SENTINEL+initial diamond tests assert NaN, should assert fn does NOT run
src/__tests__/core/semantic-audit.test.ts:760 — INVALIDATE test has no actual assertion
src/__tests__/core/semantic-audit.test.ts:596 — misleading name 'dynamicNode does NOT subscribe-time push' after fix added one
src/__tests__/core/semantic-audit.test.ts:397 — reconnect test asserts optimization as contract; update per Q3 decision
invariants_missed_this_session: composition guide §1: derived with SENTINEL dep must not compute with garbage
Writing tests against impl behavior instead of spec contract

title: Python Port Strategy
established: Protocol classes, unlimited bitmask, per-subgraph locks, core sync

title: Python-Specific Lessons Carried Forward
established: Typed Protocol classes > integer tags; unlimited-precision bitmask; per-subgraph RLock; core 100% synchronous; superset deps at construction; context managers; free-threaded Python 3.14 readiness

title: START Protocol + ROM/RAM Refactor: NodeBase, Pre-Set Dirty Mask, Rewire Buffer
topic: Clean-room redesign of NodeImpl/DynamicNodeImpl lifecycle on shared NodeBase. START handshake as tier-0 protocol message. Pre-set dirty mask for first-run gating. ROM/RAM cache semantics (state preserves, compute clears). DynamicNodeImpl rewire buffer for lazy-dep composition. Tier reshuffle (0-5). All 1426 tests passing.
decisions: START protocol message (tier 0) replaces _activating/_emittedDataDuringActivate/_connecting flags
Pre-set dirty mask unifies first-run gate and subsequent-wave logic — eliminates _everValueMask/_firstRunPending
ROM/RAM: state is ROM (preserves cache on disconnect), compute is RAM (clears cache + lastDepValues)
NodeBase abstract class with shared subscribe/sink/lifecycle machinery
DynamicNodeImpl rewire buffer (option C): buffer during rewire, detect discrepancies, re-run bounded by MAX_RERUN=16
Tier reshuffle: 0=START, 1=DIRTY/INVALIDATE, 2=PAUSE/RESUME, 3=DATA/RESOLVED, 4=COMPLETE/ERROR, 5=TEARDOWN
START-consumption-clears-dirty heuristic for operator compatibility (takeUntil, concatMap)
onMessage fallback: run fn once after connect if onMessage consumed all messages
pending status for subscribed nodes blocked on SENTINEL deps
open_questions: PY parity port needed — same refactor for node.py, dynamic_node.py, messages.py, batch.py

title: OpenClaw Context Engine Plugin: Integration Research + Testing Strategy
topic: Research into OpenClaw pluggable ContextEngine slot (v2026.3.7) as high-leverage integration for GraphReFly reactive memory. Maps memory::compact-view, scoreRelevance, packIntoBudget, stale-filter, consolidator to 3-hook interface (select, budget, compact). Existing plugin ecosystem is all static/imperative. 5-tier testing strategy: unit, integration (mock LLM), OpenClaw interface compliance, E2E quality comparison, regression. Also covers OpenHarness (HKUDS) NLAH paper findings.
decisions: OpenClaw Context Engine plugin is higher leverage than MCP Server for proving reactive memory thesis
Placed as §9.3b in Wave 2 (alongside MCP Server, not deferred to Wave 3)
3-hook mapping: select=compact-view+scoreRelevance, budget=packIntoBudget, compact=stale-filter+consolidator
No existing OpenClaw plugin has reactive propagation, budget-aware ranking, or automatic consolidation
5-tier test strategy from pure unit tests to E2E multi-turn recall comparison
OpenHarness solved-set-replacer finding: eval should track per-issue patterns, not just aggregates

title: D8 / SENTINEL Operator Initialization Fix: Protocol-Level depHasData, *Map fn→cleanup-only
topic: Fixing operator initialization fallback (D8) corruption when dep is SENTINEL (no DATA). Three failed attempts documented. Final fix: *Map operators (switchMap, exhaustMap, concatMap, mergeMap, bufferCount) fn changed to return cleanup only; accumulator operators (reduce, takeWhile, last) use protocol-level depHasData flag. PY-specific: core D8 kept for all fn-nodes (PY blocks _run_fn during _connecting), last() initial=None fix. Removed wrong null guards from evalSource/eval_source.
decisions: Three operator categories: A (*Map — fn returns cleanup), B (Timer — fn initializes timers), C (Accumulator — fn processes dep values)
Protocol-level depHasData tracking via onMessage (checks msg[0] === DATA, NOT null/undefined value check) — aligns with COMPOSITION-GUIDE §3
*Map operator fn must not call project function — only onMessage calls project on real DATA
PY core D8 fires for ALL fn-nodes (not just onMessage operators) because PY blocks _run_fn during _connecting — diverges from TS by design
PY last() must not pass initial=None when no default — None is a real value causing spurious DATA(None) push-on-subscribe
evalSource null guard (v == null) is WRONG — undefined/null are valid DATA payloads, guard removed
Single-dep DIRTY-skip optimization makes moving DATA from fn to onMessage + a.down([msg]) break two-phase protocol
invariants_enforced: No null/undefined guards as reactive gates — protocol (START/SENTINEL/DATA) is the mechanism
COMPOSITION-GUIDE §3: only SENTINEL or != null guard patterns
All operators must follow same rules — no special-casing

title: Rigor Infrastructure Plan: Property-Based Testing, TLA+ Core Spec, Executable TS↔PY Contract, and LLM Eval Cost Safety
topic: Strategic plan for hardening GraphReFly's core + extras into a semantically airtight substrate LLMs can compose on without digging into source. Motivated by V4-era self-contradicting docs and the 10x debug-cost of semantic cracks. Three rigor projects (fast-check protocol harness, tiny TLA+ core spec, TS↔PY executable contract) plus parity mitigation options besides Rust/WASM. Includes five-layer cost-safety plan for running real LLM evals without runaway token spend: fast-check invariants, replay cache, budget gate, dry-run mode, per-task caps. Full code sketches for replay cache, budget gate, and dry-run provider.
decisions: Correctness benchmark is LLM-usability, not PostgreSQL rigor — no silent corruption, visible errors, deterministic replay, no glitches/leaks
Split audit bar into Tier A (user-observable bug, fix now) and Tier B (invariant violation that works today under sync runner, defer to optimizations.md until encoded as fast-check property)

Project 1 — fast-check property-based protocol harness with 9 encoded invariants (DIRTY-before-DATA, terminal monotonicity, diamond convergence, equals substitution, START handshake, bounded-fn-per-event, bounded-subscription-count, bounded-retry-timers, bounded-pending-queue) — ~1-2 weeks, highest leverage
Project 2 — Shared .jsonl trace format + TS/PY replayers kills parity audits entirely; WAIT until graph-level audit settles so trace format doesn't need re-encoding
Project 3 — Tiny ~100-line TLA+ spec of wave protocol only (not operators/patterns); TLC exhaustively checks small graphs; becomes single source of truth for invariants

Parity mitigation ranked besides Rust/WASM: (1) executable contract traces, (2) relax contract to 'same wire protocol, idiomatic APIs', (3) single source of truth for docs, (4) TLA+ spec-driven development, (6) Rust/WASM deferred to post-1.0
Project 1 must ship before patterns/harness layer audit — Claude audits with fast-check harness in hand, not code reading alone
Rust/WASM deferred to post-1.0: doing it now locks in current design before fast-check/TLC stress-test it

LLM eval cost safety: five-layer defense (fast-check substrate invariants, replay cache, budget gate, dry-run mode, per-task caps) — Layers 2-4 unblock real evals with ~1 day of work, independent of rigor plan
Replay cache and budget gate wrapping order is load-bearing: withReplayCache(withBudgetGate(base)) so cache hits short-circuit before budget is charged (reruns are free, real calls are counted)
Six cost vectors catalogued: runaway inner subscriptions, unbounded retry, feedback storms, DIRTY amplification, unbounded rate limiter pending[], per-task prompt blow-up
costSafeEval() should be added to SESSION-mid-level-harness-blocks.md as a fifth composed block (bundles budget gate + replay cache + dry-run + per-task caps)
Pricing is currency-agnostic: use 'price' field not 'dollars', currency stored per ModelPricing, CurrencyMismatchError if models of different currencies mixed in one budget

Price tracking is best-effort, not billing reconciliation: cover input/output rates + Gemini-style threshold pricing + batchMultiplier; DEFER audio/image/video tokens, cache pricing, tool costs, storage-over-time

Don't vendor litellm's 30K+ line pricing JSON — ship ~15 curated models in default registry, expose registerModelPricing() for user extensions, suggest loadLiteLLMPricing(jsonPath) helper pattern for users who need comprehensive coverage
Authoritative safety net is maxCalls + maxInputTokens + maxOutputTokens (always computable); maxPrice is a secondary check that may be 0 for unknown models

v2 future direction — invert the API: LLMProvider exports CallStats as reactive Nodes (lastCall, totalCalls, totalInputTokens, totalOutputTokens, allCalls), pricing becomes a user-owned PricingFn attached as a derived — library ships zero pricing data, users plug in coarse/fine/litellm-backed pricing as pure functions
v1 stays in evals/lib/ scope; v2 goes into patterns.ai as observableLLMProvider() primitive — new roadmap item under §4.4 patterns.ai or §9.0c, compositionally sits alongside §9.0b mid-level blocks

v2 inversion validates the library thesis: 'library ships structured facts, users render interpretation' — pricing is the cleanest test case (precise measurement, subjective interpretation), becomes template for token usage / retrieval hits / tool-call success rate / judgment confidence exposure across all patterns.ai primitives
phasing: Phase A (user-owned, now): Finish Wave 3 extras audit + graph-level audit
Phase A.1 (parallel, user-unblocked): Replay cache + budget gate + dry-run provider (~1 day) to enable running real evals without runaway cost fear
Phase B (joint, after A): Project 1 fast-check + Project 3 TLA+ (parallel) + docs unification editing passes
Phase C (claude-owned, after B): Patterns/harness layer audit using fast-check as harness
Phase D (after B settles): Project 2 TS↔PY executable contract
Phase E (post-1.0): Rust/WASM unified core if justified
tradeoff: ~3-4 weeks on rigor infra delays the 8 differentiator blocks (resilientPipeline, graphLens.why, persistentState, agentMemory) by the same amount. Worth it if LLM-composable substrate is the public thesis. Cost-safety layers 2-4 unblock real evals in ~1 day independent of rigor plan.

title: Competitive Landscape: Self-Evolution, Multi-Agent Handoffs, 6-Block README
topic: Research sprint: Evolver (EvoMap), Hermes Agent (NousResearch), OpenAI Agents SDK, QMD, xiaohongshu structured-output post. Market convergence on self-improving agents. COMPOSITION-GUIDE §29-31 (handoff, parallel guardrail, dynamic tool selection). 6 new optimizations items. README restructured from 8 rows to 6 building blocks. Marketing §20 competitive positioning.
decisions: Market converged on closed-loop learning: Evolver (GEP solidify), Hermes (skill creation), GraphReFly (strategy model + auto-solidify)
GraphReFly already covers the substrate — gap is narrative/vocabulary, not architecture
6 building blocks for README: agentMemory, harnessLoop, guardedExecution, resilientPipeline, graphLens, persistentState
New composition patterns: §29 handoff, §30 parallel guardrail, §31 dynamic tool selection
Auto-solidify pattern: VERIFY success → catalog entry promotion
Frozen context optimization: prefix-cache-friendly prompt composition
Vocabulary to claim: auto-solidify, catalog entry, policy stack, strategy model, reactive multi-tier memory

title: Graph Module 24-Unit Review: Wave A Cleanup, Structural Simplifications, Extractions
topic: Per-function walkthrough of all of src/graph/ (24 units across graph.ts + codec.ts + profile.ts + sizeof.ts). Validated against v5 invariants. Pre-1.0 breaking simplifications identified: delete edge registry entirely (connect/disconnect/_edges), delete static factory registry, fold dumpGraph/toMermaid/toD2 into describe(format), drop toObject/toJSONString, delete dead _resolveEndpoint family. Wave A cleanup: config-driven metaPassthrough flag, typed GraphOptions, central config singleton reference, inspectorEnabled on config, graceful observe degrade. Extractions: RingBuffer<T> and sizeof to extra/utils/ (shared with reactive-log, reactive-sink, reactive data structures).
decisions: Graph layer role locked: managing (registry + lifecycle + signal) + introspection (describe/observe/diff/trace/reachable/diagrams) + persistence. Message flow is never Graph's concern — follows construction-time _deps via node subscriptions

v5 SENTINEL semantics confirmed: undefined is global sentinel, only T|null valid DATA. `.cache === undefined` IS a valid sentinel guard. Memory note updated; node.ts:247 docstring flagged for reconciliation

Delete edge registry (_edges, connect, disconnect, add forward/reverse edge scans, remove edge cleanup, fromSnapshot edge replay): empirically every call site in patterns/*.ts is redundant-with-construction; _addDep branch in connect never triggered. Replaced by edges() derived from _deps + version-counter-cached scanEdges()
Delete static factory registry (_factories, registerFactory, unregisterFactory, _factoryForPath): factories move to fromSnapshot({factories}) per-call param. Closes test-isolation hazard
Fold dumpGraph + toMermaid + toD2 into describe({format: 'pretty' | 'json' | 'mermaid' | 'd2'}): single format dispatch, shared buildDiagramModel helper
Drop toObject (alias for snapshot) and toJSONString (sugar). Keep snapshot() as primary + toJSON() as ECMAScript hook only
Central config singleton: Graph references defaultConfig (never owns its own). Delete _defaultVersioningLevel field. Keep setVersioning as thin bulk-apply helper with no default-for-future semantic
Typed GraphOptions with open index: {versioning?, config?, factories?, [key: string]: unknown}. Object.freeze(opts) in constructor
Config-driven metaPassthrough flag per message type (registerMessageType), replaces hardcoded META_FILTERED_TYPES Set that spanned three tiers
inspectorEnabled moved from Graph static to GraphReFlyConfig.inspectorEnabled (freeze-on-read). observe extras degrade gracefully instead of throwing when disabled

Extract RingBuffer<T> to extra/utils/ring-buffer.ts: three independent ring-ish implementations in repo (graph.ts RingBuffer, reactive-log NativeLogBackend inline, reactive-sink buffer.push/shift O(n) shift perf bug). Plus Unit 11 observe events would be fourth site
Extract sizeof to extra/utils/sizeof.ts with Date/RegExp/Error/URL/BigInt coverage, iterative walk (no stack overflow), try/catch per-key, shared-buffer dedup, Symbol.for('sizeof') hook

Unit 21 destroy: flush autoCheckpoint before TEARDOWN broadcast (closes data-loss gap), _destroyClearOnly runs disposers too (closes leak), aggregate errors via onError callback, Symbol.dispose support, idempotent guard, async destroy with optional flush

Promote NodeV0 from Phase 6 to Phase 3.x and wire version-counter shortcuts across Graph.diff (skip value compare when v matches), autoCheckpoint (skip redundant writes on unchanged version), fromSnapshot (no-op restore when version matches live). Per SESSION-serialization-memory-footprint.md:205 recommendation
deferred: graph.attachStorage(tiers) unification — added to docs/optimizations.md. Collapses Graph.autoCheckpoint + AutoCheckpointAdapter + extra/checkpoint.ts CheckpointAdapter + extra/cascading-cache.ts tieredStorage into one StorageTier interface with per-tier debounceMs/compactEvery/filter
Codec lazy decode + eviction policy (Unit 23 F+G) — post-1.0
Reactive-operator rewrite of autoCheckpoint (Unit 19 D) — significant refactor
Streaming snapshot (Unit 17 L, Unit 23 N) — low priority
open_questions: Producer in restore (Unit 18 O) — reactive semantics favor skipping, but audit use cases want round-trip. Needs policy call
Primary vs meta filter asymmetry in signal (Unit 9 H) — needs TEARDOWN cascade audit before symmetry
Disposer added during disposer iteration (Unit 21) — spread copy means new additions not run this round. Document or redesign
Observe event tier-surfacing gaps (Unit 11) — INVALIDATE, PAUSE, RESUME, TEARDOWN not surfaced. Any of them should be?

title: Eval Story Reframe: The Feedback Loop Is the Thesis, Not Templates
topic: Mid-§9.1 course correction. Drift: I had been framing the blog narrative as 'Treatment D proves templates are necessary.' Actual thesis: the eval → diagnose → fix-in-library → re-eval loop is the publishable product. A/B/C/D are stations traversing the loop, not a scoreboard. C (auto-refine) is load-bearing for the thesis — previously treated as skippable. Templates reframed as loop output, not headline. Also: verified Claude pricing/limits from docs.anthropic.com (opus 4.6 is $5/$25, not $15/$75 — our registry was 3x stale; Tier 1 Sonnet is 50 RPM, 30K ITPM, 8K OTPM). Eight cost-tracking / cache / rate-limit / crash-recovery bugs found and fixed during execution. Three publishable findings surfaced by the eval itself: shifting-failure pattern, reasoning-loop cap exhaustion, validator-vs-judge disagreement.
decisions: Blog narrative is the self-improvement loop, not the A→D score progression
Treatment C is load-bearing; wire llmCompose in or extract the refine loop externally
Treatment D's templates are loop-discovered output, not the thesis
Defer paid Sonnet runs — glm-4.7 data is mechanically sufficient; one publish-tier run when ready to publish, for credibility anchor only
Claude opus-4-6 pricing updated $15/$75 → $5/$25 in registry (3x stale)
Anthropic rate limits in registry switched from 40K/80K/100K TPM to Tier 1 ITPM (30K Sonnet+Opus, 50K Haiku)
Cost-tracking bug: generation calls had req.model=undefined so estimateTokenCost returned 0; only judge calls showed cost. Fixed in contrastive.ts
Reasoning tokens were uncounted: outputTokens = completion_tokens missed usage.completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens. For GLM-5.1 that's ~50% undercount. Fixed in OpenAICompatibleProvider
mergeRuns double-counted total_cost_usd on resume (prev.cost + current.cost where current included cached tasks). Now recomputes from deduped task array
Cache key decoupled from wrapper chain naming via ReplayCacheOptions.providerKey — adding/reordering inner wrappers no longer silently invalidates cache
Dry-run / real cache isolation: providerKey = provider+dryrun when EVAL_MODE=dry-run, prevents cross-mode poisoning
Registry-resolved maxOutputTokens baked into cache key salt so bumping the cap invalidates stale truncated responses
Rate limiter moved INSIDE cache wrapper (cache → budget → limiter → base): cache hits short-circuit before pacing
Per-task try/catch in runContrastiveEval: transient API errors record a diagnostic failure and the run continues
Anti-pattern to avoid: wrapper chains where cache-relevant identity depends on inner.name. Use explicit providerKey.
findings_for_blog: Shifting-failure pattern: A→B→D on glm-4.7 kept error rate in 13→10→7% band but which specific tasks failed kept shuffling — prompt structure redistributes failures, failure surface is a prompt function not a fixed task/model property

Reasoning-loop cap exhaustion: dominant failure mode on thinking models. Model exhausts output cap on reasoning tokens, emits empty visible content. Latency up to 16 minutes observed. Implication: evals on reasoning models need bigger output caps OR reasoning disabled

Validator vs judge disagreement: on multi-step-user-signup D, judges pass the output as valid JSON with correct behaviors; validateSpec rejects because LLM invented template params ($fn, $config) the spec doesn't support. Real tension between behavioral judgment and structural validation — worth investigation
open_questions: Wire Treatment C via llmCompose (~50 LOC) or document runner's B-C equivalence?
Cross-provider eval workflow (Sonnet eval + GPT-4.1 judge): currently untested
Reasoning-token cap handling: raise to 131K or add EVAL_REASONING_MODE=off?
Publish-tier: one model or two for the blog credibility anchor?

title: Blog Materials: GraphReFly Eval-Driven Catalog Loop (§9.1 Wave 1)
topic: Pre-blog-draft materials consolidating everything needed to write the Wave 1 announcement post on the feedback-loop thesis. 4 runs complete on glm-4.7 (A,B,C,D), 120 total task results, cross-treatment task stability matrix, three publishable findings (shifting failures, reasoning-loop cap exhaustion, judge-vs-validator disagreement), cost-accuracy bug history, publish-tier run plan on openai/gpt-5.3-codex + openai/gpt-5.4-mini judge. Companion to SESSION-eval-story-reframe.md (thesis reframe) — this file is the materials pack.
decisions: Thesis: the feedback loop is the product, NOT any individual treatment
4-treatment loop stations: A baseline, B auto-prompt, C auto-refine, D loop-discovered templates
Keep Treatment C in the blog even though refine never triggered on glm-4.7 — the 'never triggered' is itself a finding (F4: refine utility is model-family-dependent)
Publish-tier plan: gpt-5.3-codex (non-thinking, coding-specialized) as eval, gpt-5.4-mini as judge (cross-family, cheap). Skip B on publish-tier unless narrative needs it. Est. $10-15 for A+C+D.
maxOutputTokens=128K verified in registry for gpt-5.3-codex (both direct and openrouter routes) — Codex is non-thinking so cap won't be the binding constraint
30 of 30 tasks pass functions treatment in every run; graphspec pass varies 25-27 of 30. 21 tasks pass everywhere, 9 shift treatment-to-treatment, 0 fail in all four
error-circuit-breaker is the only task where Treatment D's resilientFetch template monotonically fixed it (A,B,C all failed; D passed) — the cleanest 'templates work for X specific gap' story
Blog should NOT frame as 'D beats A' — that's the drift we just corrected

Publishable findings (3): F1 shifting-failure pattern, F2 reasoning-loop cap exhaustion, F3 validator-vs-judge disagreement. Plus F4 (refine utility is model-family-dependent) as a hook for publish-tier Codex comparison
run_totals: C (auto-refine): $1.37, 25/30 graphspec (stricter validity criterion — refine never triggered)
blog_outline: Hook — paint the eval problem and self-improving-loop framing
4-treatment experiment — what each tests in the loop
What we learned (findings F1, F2, F3) — heart of the post
Reproducibility — one-liner commands, pointer to CHEAP-AND-SAFE.md
What the loop does next — shipped fixes (templates, conditionalMap, median op, llmScore desc)
Open threads — F4 (refine utility), judge/validator tension, prompt-structure-shapes-failures
Close — fork it, run it, if failures surprise you that's the signal
blog_quotable_numbers: 60 tasks × 4 treatments on one model, under $7 total
30% of tasks failed in ≥1 treatment, 0% in all four
One task (error-circuit-breaker) that only the resilientFetch template fixed
16-minute latency on one failure — the model spent its whole output budget thinking
8 bugs in our own runner discovered by trying to trust the numbers
open_questions: Run publish-tier Codex A+C+D now or wait for fresh session?
Should B also run on publish-tier?
Judge-vs-validator tension (F3): dedicated follow-up post or paragraph in main?
Reasoning-loop finding (F2): spin into separate 'evaluating thinking models' post?
Ship scorecard/latest.json before or after the main blog lands?
remaining_before_wave1_ships: Design-partner outreach emails (20-30 targets, per marketing strategy §16A)

title: Strategy Review, Roadmap Reprioritization & Demo Realignment
topic: Post-小红书 strategy reflection triggered by a post on agent infra commoditization. Analyzed all strategy docs. Identified explainability-first (explainPath) as P0 differentiator. Concluded Wave 1 eval story is valid but does not leverage the differentiator — eval and explainPath are sequential layers (compile-time vs runtime). Added §9.3e Spending Alerts demo to Wave 2 (backs homepage 02, proves explainPath, CLI CTA, no GIF required). Sharpened Demo 0 and Demo 6 framings. Added homepage demo link inventory to Wave 3 deliverables. Updated profile README (persistentState → Graph.attachStorage, removed proposed auto-solidify). Added Wave 1→2 bridge sentence to marketing strategy §15. Clarified knowledgeGraph() application: internal schema nodes (entities/edges/adjacency) vs user-defined TEntity/TRelation types; primary use is structured LLM memory, secondary is reactive domain model.
decisions: GraphReFly is NOT in the commoditized agent infra layer — reactive state coherence, explainPath, reduction layer, and human governance gate have low commoditization risk
Wave 1 eval story (catalog quality) is valid but generic; explainPath (Wave 2) is the P0 differentiator that no competitor has
Eval and explainability are sequential not parallel: validateSpec failures happen at compile-time, explainPath is a runtime tool — do not merge them into Wave 1
Codex eval plan: run A (already running) + C (~$5, proves F4 refine-utility hypothesis on non-thinking model), skip D (template story already complete on GLM data)

Added §9.3e Spending Alerts demo to Wave 2: minimal fromTimer→anomalyDetector→flagNode pipeline, CLI CTA for blog, MCP CTA for Claude Code story, examples/spending-alerts/ + docs page, backs homepage 02
Demo 0 sharpened to pain-point-first: email scattered across Gmail/Slack/Linear → one graph → explain every flag; closes homepage 01 AND 02 via explainPath step
Demo 6 rewrote: now explicitly self-improving + explainable showcase — agent fails → explainPath shows causal chain → REFLECT updates strategy model → next run avoids failure
Homepage demo link inventory added to Wave 3: Email Triage → Demo 0 (Wave 3), Spending Alerts → §9.3e (Wave 2), Knowledge Graph → static quickstart (Wave 2)
Scorecard metric order: causal trace completeness moved to first position (was last) — it is the P0 differentiator
Profile README: persistentState() superseded by Graph.attachStorage(), auto-solidify removed (proposed not shipped)
Marketing strategy §15: blog close now includes Wave 1→2 bridge sentence
knowledgeGraph() primary application is structured LLM memory (entities/relationships extracted from LLM outputs, available for context retrieval), not user-facing knowledge organization UI
demo_priority_order: P1: Demo 0 Email Triage (Wave 3, backs homepage 01+02)
P2: Demo 6 Agent Observatory enhanced (Wave 3, harness story)

title: Google Cloud Next '26, ClawSweeper, Graph vs ReAct Analysis
topic: Three-signal competitive analysis: (1) Google Cloud Next '26 — Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, Agent Identity, native MCP, Apple partnership; validates commoditization risk assessment, 'harness' no longer a claimable category. (2) ClawSweeper — OpenClaw AI maintenance bot (50 parallel Codex, 4000 issues/day), two-phase Review/Apply architecture maps to GraphReFly's TRIAGE→GATE→EXECUTE pattern, file system as ad-hoc reactive graph. (3) Graph vs ReAct debate — LangGraph DX complaints conflated with graph-architecture criticism; data shows graph agents outperform ReAct (95% vs 85% accuracy); GraphReFly positioned for cases where ReAct is insufficient (fan-out, governance, causal trace).
decisions: 'Harness' becomes a descriptive word, not a claimable category — Wave 2 headline shifts to causal explainability + protocol-level coordination
MCP server shifts from differentiator to distribution channel; value is what's exposed through it (explainPath, observe)
Cross-cloud agent identity is a vacuum — GraphReFly's protocol-level ABAC is inherently cloud-agnostic
Composition success rate target more urgent — market tolerance for 'experimental' dropping
ClawSweeper's Review/Apply = TRIAGE→GATE→EXECUTE independently validated
ClawSweeper demo potential noted but NOT added to roadmap — needs further design
Graph vs ReAct: LangGraph's DX failure ≠ graph-architecture failure; GraphReFly addresses DX via sugar constructors + GraphSpec
New reply marketing keywords: agent identity cross-cloud, agent governance vendor lock-in, Google Agent Platform limitations

title: Harness Engineering Trends & GraphReFly Positioning
topic: Web research (30+ sources) on harness engineering wave triggered by xiaohongshu Claude Code deconstruction video. Three-trend framework: (1) harness self-evolution as foundation, (2) Ashby's Law / topology as variety-reduction as pursuit, (3) thinner-is-better as result. David's 0.99→1.0 thesis (isolated failure domains + boundary resilience). Four consensus-level building block candidates. Claude Code harness ↔ GraphReFly primitive mapping. Core narrative: GraphReFly reduces dimensionality, not adds constraints.
decisions: Three-trend framework: self-evolution=foundation, Ashby's Law=pursuit, thinner=result
David's 0.99→1.0 thesis is the most precise GraphReFly value proposition to date
rewire() API gap identified but parked — disconnect/resubscribe needs test coverage first
Building block design deferred to post-worktree-walkthrough patterns reconsolidation
Post-1.0 changesets (op-log, lazy decode) NOT needed for trend 1 foundation — diff+add+remove sufficient
SubscriptionGraph is not a tier manager — three-tier context is a topology design pattern
Intent classifier as derived node: graph role vs internal implementation are separate concerns
harnessProfile() as harness hygiene tool (like code coverage) — key deliverable for trend 3

title: Patterns & Extras Consolidation Plan: Two-Layer Separation, Promotion, Reorganization, Naming, Scope Boundaries
topic: Post-audit '揉碎' (break-apart-and-reform) of the exported API surface. Core principle: building blocks (orthogonal primitives in index.ts) vs presets (opinionated combinations in presets.ts). Promotion list (domainMeta, keepalive, mutation framework, decay, budgetGate → extra/). Full extra/ folder restructure (operators/, sources/, io/, resilience/, data-structures/, storage/, composition/, mutation/, worker/). 7 naming convention rules. Scope boundaries per folder (charter, what goes in/doesn't, boundary test). 5-phase implementation sequencing.
decisions: Two-layer separation: index.ts=building blocks, presets.ts=sugar. Boundary test: can users still build the same thing if preset is removed?
Promotion criteria: used by 2+ unrelated pattern domains with zero domain-specific semantics → extra/
Candidates promoted: domainMeta, keepalive, lightMutation/wrapMutation, BaseAuditRecord/createAuditLog, tryIncrementBounded, decay, budgetGate
extra/ reorganized into 9 folders: operators, sources, io, resilience, data-structures, storage, composition, mutation, worker
7 naming rules: layer prefix, gate-family disambiguation, error-handling family, collection family, LLM-call family, scoring/feedback family, disambiguation
Each folder gets charter + what goes in + what doesn't + naming convention + boundary test
5-phase implementation: Phase 1 (promote+move), Phase 2 (rename), Phase 3 (folder restructure), Phase 4 (preset extraction), Phase 5 (doc+export cleanup)

title: AI / Harness Module 24-Unit Review (in progress)
topic: Per-function walkthrough of src/patterns/ai/ (~3,778 LOC single file plus 31-file adapters subtree) and src/patterns/harness/ (9 files, ~2,111 LOC), with refine-loop, graphspec, and surface as Wave C adjacents. Mandatory per-unit explainability / topology-check against describe()/observe()/explain() to catch islands caused by imperative anti-patterns. Validates the 6-blocks proposal (agentMemory, harnessLoop, guardedExecution, resilientPipeline, graphLens, Graph.attachStorage) and the 7-stage harness loop (INTAKE→TRIAGE→QUEUE→GATE→EXECUTE→VERIFY→REFLECT) against v5 spec invariants, COMPOSITION-GUIDE §7/§28/§32, and SESSION-strategy-roadmap-demo-reprioritization (composition success rate >95% target).
planning_decisions: Wave 0 first: split ai/index.ts into folder-shape (prompts/, agents/, memory/, context/) via codemod before per-unit review — same pattern used in §9.3d resolved 2026-04-22
Two-session cadence: Wave A (units 1-14, AI primitives + adapters) and Wave B+C (units 15-24, harness + refine-loop + graphspec + surface)
Mandatory explainability criterion per unit: minimal composition + describe(ascii/mermaid) + explain() must show clean causal chain, zero islands. Fix = reactive edges, not imperative emits
6-blocks README proposal (agentMemory/harnessLoop/guardedExecution/resilientPipeline/graphLens/attachStorage) is open for reshape during review
Topology check candidate for shipped utility: validateNoIslands(graph) companion to validateGraphObservability
drift_hotlist: ai/index.ts at 3778 LOC in one file (graph.ts pre-review symptom)
27 new-Promise / AbortController / setTimeout occurrences inside patterns/ai/index.ts (vs 0 in graph.ts post-review)
harness/strategy.ts exposes mutable _map through closure — callers mutate via record()/lookup() outside the reactive graph
agentLoop._currentAbortController + statusNode.emit() inside effect bodies — imperative coordination in reactive cladding
P3 .cache-in-fn-body re-pass needed over 3,778 LOC (last audit predates current size)
Hardcoded message-type checks not yet scrubbed against messageTier utility in ai/harness
open_items_from_optimizations: refineLoop.setSeed/reset persistent re-seed for cross-item learning
Domain-level for/await in strategies — TopicGraph+cursor alternative
EXECUTE actuators + VERIFY re-eval (closing the dogfood loop)
autoSolidify (VERIFY success → catalog entry promotion) proposed

title: Graph Narrow-Waist: Pattern-Author API & Inspection Consolidation
topic: Root cause analysis of development velocity stagnation: no narrow waist between protocol layer (11 message types, two-phase push, diamond resolution, batch coalescing) and pattern layer (16 domains, 120k LOC). 65 protocol-level drops audited across all pattern domains. 4 new Graph methods (graph.derived with keepAlive, graph.effect, graph.produce, graph.batch) absorb ~85% of drops. Inspection consolidated from 7 methods to 3 (describe=topology, observe=data, profile=resources). Composition guide split into 4 level-based files (protocol, graph, patterns, solutions). Fast-check properties P1-P7 planned for Graph-level contract verification.
decisions: 4 new Graph methods: derived (with keepAlive option), effect, produce, batch — named to match underlying primitives exactly
No separate graph.link() method — graph.derived with keepAlive replaces closure-mirror pattern (COMPOSITION-GUIDE §28) entirely
keepAlive only on graph.derived — effects don't need it (run for side effect), producers don't care (own lifecycle)
Each method absorbs: path resolution, SENTINEL guard, registration, disposal internally — no individual disposer exposure
Inspection consolidation: explain() folds into describe(), reachable() folds into describe(), trace() extracted to inspect/ addon, get() removed (0 callers)
Collapse: setAll (0 callers), removeAll (rare sugar), get (0 callers)
Reclassify as escape hatches: node(), resolve(), error(), complete(), invalidate(), signal()
Pattern-level utils stay where they are: lightMutation/wrapMutation in extra/mutation/, processManager in patterns/process/ — correctly placed at Level 3
CQRS error classes move from patterns/_internal/errors.ts to patterns/cqrs/errors.ts
Composition guide splits into 4 files: protocol (Level 0), graph (Level 1), patterns (Level 2), solutions (Level 3 — not final highest level)
Implementer escalation protocol: new node usage patterns that don't fit the 4 methods must be raised as 9-question design review units, not silently forced or worked around
open_questions: graph.effect() set() helper — should callback receive set(path, value) to stay inside waist?
Dep typing with string paths — defer until core derivedT lands?
graph.produce() fromAny vs singleFromAny dispatch decision

title: Human/LLM Intervention Primitives — Hub-First Architecture
topic: Mapping the four-class error taxonomy (transient / LLM-self-correctable / user-only / structural) onto graphrefly, then deep into the user-only intervention surface where current pipelineGraph.gate() only supports binary approve/reject. Multiple over-engineered framings (5 specialized humanInput factories; humanInput/approvalGate/valve as 3 primitives) collapsed onto a hub-first architecture: messaging hub + standard Message envelope (with responseSchema field, AG-UI-inspired) + valve as the only true primitives, with humanInput/approvalGate/tracker as sibling presets on the substrate. Inline editing, steering, parameter changes, model swaps, and stream cancellation all unify as derived+switchMap usage patterns over the same substrate. Park-without-halt and next-boundary-injection share the same topic+cursor mechanism, differing only in consumer cadence.
decisions: Two true primitives: hub+envelope (with schema field) and valve (with adapter abort hookup gap). Everything else is sibling preset or composition pattern.
approvalGate is NOT a specialization of humanInput — they are siblings on the same substrate with different roles, initiators, and public return shapes (Node<T> vs GateController).

One humanInput primitive instead of 5 specializations (humanInput/Choice/Edit/Feedback/Approve-with-edits). Schema in envelope (AG-UI-inspired responseSchema) lets LLM and UI both consume the same shape declaration. UX-coherent (one chat box, LLM phrases the question).
Steering, inline edit, parameter change, and model swap all unify as derived([editableInputs]) + switchMap over the LLM call. The taxonomy of 'what changed' is irrelevant to the topology.
Park-as-deferred and next-boundary-injection share the same hub+topic+cursor mechanism. Tracker reads continuously; LLM samples on generationDone signal. Same topic, different cursor strategies.

Reactive model gets parallel interrupts, cascading cancellation, ambient/async, agent steering, sub-agent isolation, TTL — for free as composition. AG-UI/LangGraph each require explicit protocol-level features.
Standard topic naming convention: prompts / responses / injections / deferred. Not meta-tag-based discovery — UI subscribes to well-known topic.

AG-UI vs A2UI distinction: AG-UI is wire transport (event protocol over SSE/WS); A2UI is generative UI declaration format (JSON widget tree). Postal service vs letter-in-envelope. graphrefly translates 1:1 to AG-UI events; A2UI is a later wave.

Adapter AbortController hookup is the highest-priority follow-up gap — shared by panic button, switchMap-driven cancellation, inline editing, and any reactive cancellation path. Currently valve cuts propagation but in-flight LLM HTTP keeps generating tokens (cost continues).
open_questions: Should humanInput and approvalGate literally share an envelope publish path or just conform independently?
Where does responseSchema validation happen — at publish, receipt, or both?
Is tracker better named parkedQueue or deferredTracker to disambiguate from harness retrospective dogfood?
Should boundaryDrain be a named factory or stay a documented recipe (bufferWhen + generationDone)?
Adapter abort: universal contract change (pre-1.0 breaking) or optional capability flag?
real_gaps: Adapter AbortController hookup (shared by valve, switchMap, inline edit, panic)
bufferWhen(notifier) operator (verify if existing buffer family covers it)
Standard topic naming preset in patterns/messaging
Harness closed-loop wiring (pre-existing project_harness_closed_loop_gap, blocks end-to-end type-2 error story)
